name: Handspring Vault
description: The front handspring vault is one of the first real vaults gymnasts learn. Your daughter runs down the runway, jumps on the springboard, places her hands on the vault table, and pushes off to land on her feet facing the judges. Think of it like a very fast, powered cartwheel over the table.
whatToLookFor: A fast, confident run down the runway — speed makes the whole vault easier
A big jump onto the springboard with both feet together
Straight arms when her hands hit the vault table — bent arms waste the push
A strong push off the table so she's in the air for a visible moment
A controlled landing with slightly bent knees, not a crash or stumble
commonMistakes: Slowing down on the approach — this is very common and makes the vault harder, not easier
Bending the arms on the table, which collapses the vault and looks messy to judges
Landing flat-footed with a thud instead of absorbing the impact with bent knees
Not pushing tall enough off the table, making the flight phase look short

quickTipsChecklist.description: She builds up good speed the whole way down the runway
Arms are locked straight when hands touch the vault
She pushes hard off the table and rises into the air
She lands with bent knees and doesn't take extra steps

videos.title: Front Handspring Vault — Tutorial

name: Tsukahara Vault (Tuck)
description: Named after Japanese gymnast Mitsuo Tsukahara, this vault involves a half-turn onto the table followed by a back salto (backflip) in the air. Your daughter approaches the vault, jumps to place her hands while turning sideways, then launches off into a backflip and lands facing the vault. It's a big step up from basic handspring vaults.
whatToLookFor: A powerful run and jump — speed is even more critical here than in basic vaults
A clear half-turn as she places her hands on the table
Her body going up and over in a visible arc — you should see her rise before she drops
A tight tuck position during the backflip (knees pulled to chest)
Opening up before landing so she lands on her feet, not still spinning
commonMistakes: Not getting enough height off the table, making the backflip rushed and incomplete
A loose tuck position — legs dangling instead of pulled tight to the chest
Landing too close to the vault, which means she didn't get enough distance off the table
Stepping backward on landing because she didn't open up soon enough from the tuck

quickTipsChecklist.description: Full sprint all the way to the board
The sideways twist onto the table is visible and controlled
Knees pulled firmly to chest during the flip
She opens her body and spots the landing before she gets there

videos.title: Tsukahara Vault — Gymnastics Tutorial

name: Yurchenko Layout
description: The Yurchenko Layout is the standard Yurchenko vault that Level 7-8 gymnasts compete. Your daughter performs the distinctive Yurchenko entry — a round-off onto the springboard followed by a back handspring onto the vault table — then launches off in a completely straight body position (no tucking or bending) and backflips to land. The "layout" refers to the straight, fully extended body in the air after the table. Almost all elite vault families use this same entry, making this vault the gateway to the entire Yurchenko world.
whatToLookFor: A smooth, fast round-off onto the springboard — the timing here is crucial
An explosive push off the table in a straight body position
Her body rising high above the vault before beginning any rotation
The landing: two feet, slightly bent knees, arms coming down naturally
commonMistakes: A slow or poorly timed round-off that kills all the speed before the board
Piking (bending at the hips) over the table instead of staying straight
Not pushing hard enough off the table, resulting in a flat, low arc
Landing with straight legs (stiff knees) which is hard on the joints

quickTipsChecklist.description: The round-off feeds speed into the springboard
No visible hip bend as she goes over the table
She rises clearly above the vault before coming down
Knees bend to absorb the landing

videos.title: Yurchenko Vault — Tutorial and Drills

name: Yurchenko with Twist
description: Building on the basic Yurchenko, this vault adds a full 360-degree twist while your daughter is in the air. After the round-off onto the board and the push off the vault table, she performs one full twist before landing. It looks spectacular and is a significant achievement for a competitive gymnast.
whatToLookFor: The same powerful Yurchenko entry as the basic version
Her body going tall and straight off the table before the twist begins
A clean, single twist — the full turn should happen at the peak of her height
Landing facing away from the vault (correct for a full twist)
No steps on landing — this is a high value skill so deductions hurt
commonMistakes: Starting the twist too early before getting height off the table
An incomplete or uneven twist — a 'half and a half' instead of one clean full
Losing body tension mid-twist so she looks floppy in the air
Taking multiple steps on landing because the twist timing was off

quickTipsChecklist.description: She gets up before starting to twist
One complete 360° turn, clearly visible
No looseness during the twist
Lands with minimal extra steps

videos.title: Yurchenko Full Twist Vault

name: Amanar Vault
description: Named after Romanian legend Simona Amanar, this is one of the most impressive vaults in women's gymnastics. It uses the Yurchenko entry but adds two and a half twists in the air — an incredible amount of spin for such a short time. This is the vault Simone Biles made famous and is considered a hallmark of elite gymnastics.
whatToLookFor: An explosive, maximum-speed Yurchenko entry
Remarkable height off the table — she needs time to complete all those twists
Multiple twists happening rapidly but in control
A clean stick or near-stick on landing — judges expect excellence from elite vaults
Her chest staying up on landing, not hunching forward
commonMistakes: Not generating enough power in the approach, making the vault visibly rushed
Losing count on the twists and under-rotating (an over-rotation can be dangerous)
Body going soft during the twists, which slows rotation unpredictably
Landing with feet wide apart instead of together

quickTipsChecklist.description: Full sprint, maximum height off board
Arms and body stay tight throughout every twist
She rises significantly above the vault level
Lands in balance, chest up, minimal steps

videos.title: Amanar Vault — Simone Biles Competition

name: Cheng Vault
description: The Cheng vault is astonishing to watch. Your daughter does a round-off onto the springboard, then places her hands on the table with a half-turn (so she's now facing the runway she came from), then pushes off into a front pike salto. It combines backward and forward rotations in one vault, and very few gymnasts in the world can do it cleanly.
whatToLookFor: The distinctive round-off + half twist onto the table — she should be facing backward
An explosive push-off into the front salto
A tight pike position with straight legs and hands reaching toward toes
Enough height to open up from the pike before landing
A landing facing the judges (since she flipped forward)
commonMistakes: Not rotating enough on the half-twist onto the table — the timing is extremely complex
Insufficient height, making the front flip look rushed
Loose pike position — bent knees or hands not reaching toes
Over-rotating and landing hunched forward

quickTipsChecklist.description: Clear half-twist onto the table is visible
She gets up high before the flip begins
Legs straight, hands toward toes
Opens from pike in time, lands in balance

videos.title: Cheng Vault — Gymnastics

name: Cartwheel onto Springboard
description: This is one of the earliest vault-preparation skills your daughter will learn. She cartwheels onto the springboard to develop coordination and timing before learning full vaults. It's a training skill rather than a competition vault on its own, but mastering it builds confidence for the bigger skills to come.
whatToLookFor: Straight arms during the cartwheel — a bent arm cartwheel is a bad habit to form early
Hitting the springboard at the correct spot (center, near the front)
Confidence and commitment — hesitation makes the skill harder
A clean cartwheel shape, not a crooked or sideways one
commonMistakes: Crooked or lopsided cartwheels — her hands and feet should be in a straight line
Bending the arms when hands hit the board
Stopping or hesitating before the cartwheel, losing momentum
Not looking where she's going on the approach

quickTipsChecklist.description: Arms stay locked throughout the cartwheel
Hands make contact at the right spot
No hesitation on the run-up

videos.title: Beginner Vault Skills and Springboard Tutorial

name: Straddle Jump over Vault
description: An early vault skill where your daughter approaches the table, places her hands, and straddles over (legs apart to the sides) before landing. It teaches her to trust the springboard and develop the arm strength and coordination needed for real vaults. Common in recreational gymnastics and early competitive levels.
whatToLookFor: Arms pushing off the table with good strength
Legs going wide out to the sides in a straddle — not in front
Clear height off the table before straddling
Landing with both feet at the same time
commonMistakes: Not pushing off the table hard enough — the push is what gives her height
A weak straddle where the legs barely spread
Landing one foot at a time (should be two feet simultaneously)
Looking down at the ground instead of ahead on landing

quickTipsChecklist.description: Arms push hard to create height
Legs spread clearly to each side
Both feet hit the mat at the same time

videos.title: Straddle Over Vault — Beginner Gymnastics

name: Handspring with Half Twist
description: A step up from the basic handspring vault, this version adds a half turn in the air so your daughter lands facing the vault table instead of away from it. It requires more coordination and body awareness than the basic handspring but is achievable for recreational and early competitive gymnasts.
whatToLookFor: The same strong handspring onto and off the table as the basic version
A half twist that happens at the peak of her height — not too early, not too late
Landing facing the vault (not sideways or turned too far)
Arms coming down to help control the landing
commonMistakes: Starting the twist before leaving the table — it should happen in the air
An incomplete half twist, landing at 45 degrees instead of fully facing the vault
Losing arm control during the twist, which wobbles the body
Landing too close to the vault because she did not push hard enough off the table

quickTipsChecklist.description: Leaves table fully before beginning the twist
Fully faces the vault on landing
Arms stay close to body during twist for control

videos.title: Handspring Half Twist Vault Tutorial

name: Yurchenko Pike
description: Instead of staying straight (layout) in the air, this vault features the Yurchenko entry followed by a tight pike (sharp 90-degree bend at the hips with straight legs). The pike position makes the rotation faster but requires tremendous hamstring flexibility and body control. It is more advanced than a basic Yurchenko.
whatToLookFor: The Yurchenko round-off entry looking powerful and timed correctly
A sharp fold at the hips into the pike — legs should be nearly touching her chest
Straight knees throughout the pike position (bent knees reduce the score)
Opening out of the pike in time to land on her feet
Feet together on landing
commonMistakes: A 'soft' pike where the legs are angled instead of sharply folded
Bending the knees inside the pike position
Not opening from the pike in time, causing an awkward landing
Piking too late — should fold immediately after leaving the table

quickTipsChecklist.description: Hips fold to 90 degrees or less
Legs are completely straight throughout
Body opens before landing is reached

videos.title: Yurchenko Pike Vault — Gymnastics

name: Glide Kip
description: The kip is the most fundamental bars skill in gymnastics — it's the entry point to everything else on uneven bars. Your daughter hangs from the bar, swings forward (glide), then whips her feet up to the bar and pushes down to rise to a front support position (sitting on top of the bar with straight arms). Every bars routine starts with a kip or ends with one.
whatToLookFor: A big, long glide forward — the longer the glide, the more power for the kip
Toes reaching up to touch the bar as her body swings back
Her hips shooting upward in one quick, powerful motion
Ending up with straight arms and her hips resting on the bar
No piking too early — the timing of the hip push is everything
commonMistakes: A short glide that doesn't generate enough momentum
Pulling with the arms instead of using the hip push — the kip is mostly a hip skill
Feet going to the bar too slowly, losing all the momentum
Ending with bent arms instead of pushing to a full straight-arm support
Head dipping (looking down) which breaks the body line

quickTipsChecklist.description: Swings way out before the hips come up
Toes zip up to the bar, not slowly
The upward motion comes from the hips, not pulling
Arrives at the bar with locked, straight arms

videos.title: How to Do a Kip on Bars — Step by Step Tutorial
Kip Drills on Bars — Swing Big

name: Cast to Handstand
description: A cast is when your daughter swings her hips away from the bar while holding it — like a swing on a playground. A cast to handstand means she swings so high that she reaches a perfect vertical (handstand) position above the bar. This skill is required in every level of gymnastics from about Level 4 upward and is the foundation for almost every bars connection.
whatToLookFor: Starting from a hollow body position before the cast
Her hips shooting UP and AWAY from the bar — not just back
Achieving a true vertical position (handstand) above the bar
Legs staying together and straight throughout
Controlled return back to the bar — not just falling
commonMistakes: Only casting to 45 degrees or horizontal instead of all the way to vertical
Legs splitting apart during the cast — they should stay squeezed together
Piking at the hips (bending at the waist) on the way up
Arching the back instead of maintaining a straight body line
Rushing back to the bar without controlling the descent

quickTipsChecklist.description: Hips push upward, not just backward
Achieves true handstand above the bar
No split between the legs throughout the skill
No arch or pike — straight from shoulders to toes

videos.title: Cast to Handstand on Bars — Tutorial

name: Back Hip Circle
description: Your daughter starts sitting on top of the bar (in a front support), then tips forward and rotates backwards around the bar in a full circle, ending back where she started. Her hips stay in contact with the bar the whole way around. It looks like a slow-motion spin around the bar and is usually one of the first bars skills gymnasts learn.
whatToLookFor: A tight, hollow body — abs engaged, back not arching
Hips staying in contact with the bar throughout the spin
A controlled, smooth rotation — not a panicked, flailing spin
Ending back in the front support position with straight arms
Head staying neutral — not looking down or throwing the head back
commonMistakes: Letting the hips pull away from the bar during the rotation (this stops the spin)
Arching the back during the circle instead of staying tight and hollow
Bending the arms, which makes the whole skill fall apart
Going too fast or panicking halfway through and letting go
Landing back on top of the bar with bent arms

quickTipsChecklist.description: Belly stays in contact with the bar all the way around
Core engaged, no arching or piking
Arms stay straight and strong throughout
Controlled circle, not a panic spin

videos.title: Back Hip Circle — Beginner Bars Tutorial

name: Giant Swing
description: A giant is a full 360-degree swing around the bar while hanging with straight arms and a stretched body. Your daughter starts in a handstand above the bar and swings all the way around, passing through the lowest point (hanging below the bar) and back up to handstand again. Giants are the most important skill on bars — they generate the power for releases, pirouettes, and dismounts.
whatToLookFor: A long, stretched body shape throughout the entire swing
An obvious hollow position as she comes through the bottom of the swing
The handstand at the top looking solid and vertical
Arms staying straight — any bent arms receive an automatic deduction
No piking at the hips — her body should look like one long line
commonMistakes: A 'banana' back (arch) instead of a hollow body position
Bending the arms at any point, especially at the top going into handstand
Piking at the hips in the bottom of the swing
Shoulders creeping up to the ears instead of staying down and strong
Losing the body line coming up on the back side of the swing

quickTipsChecklist.description: From fingertips to toes she looks tall and tight
Core engaged as she swings through the lowest point
Elbows locked throughout the entire giant
Reaches a true handstand at the top

videos.title: Giant Swing on Bars — Gymnastics Tutorial

name: Tkachev Release
description: Named after Soviet gymnast Alexander Tkachev (though now performed by women too), this is a dramatic release move. Your daughter swings forward, releases the bar, flies backward in a straddle position (legs apart) over the bar, and then re-catches the bar. It looks like she's flying through the air above the bar with her legs spread wide. Parents often gasp at this one.
whatToLookFor: A powerful cast to handstand before the skill
A clear release of the bar — both hands let go simultaneously
A wide, clean straddle position while she's flying over the bar
The bar passing between her legs (she flies over it straddled)
A confident re-catch — both hands grabbing the bar at the same time
commonMistakes: Not having enough swing to get over the bar — an underpowered Tkachev is scary to watch
A narrow straddle (legs not wide enough) that causes her to hit the bar
Catching only one hand or catching late
Losing the body shape in the air — she should maintain control throughout
Looking down during the catch instead of at where her hands are going

quickTipsChecklist.description: Full handstand cast before the skill
Legs spread wide, bar passes between them cleanly
Both hands let go at the same moment
Both hands catch the bar at the same time

videos.title: Tkachev Release — Gymnastics Bars

name: Pirouette (Bar Turn)
description: A pirouette on bars is a turn in the handstand position at the top of the bar. After casting to a handstand, your daughter pirouettes (turns) either a half-turn (180°) or a full turn (360°) while balancing in the handstand. The half-pirouette is used to change direction or transfer between skills, while the full pirouette is a difficulty element in itself. Parents often see these as the brief pause-and-spin at the top of the bar swing.
whatToLookFor: A solid handstand position before the turn begins
The turn happening at the very top of the swing, not on the way up
Staying tight and in handstand throughout the turn — not wobbling or dipping
Clean connection into the next skill after the turn
Both hands on the bar at all times (she releases one to turn, then catches again)
commonMistakes: Starting the turn too early, before reaching handstand — creates an ugly, incomplete turn
Collapsing out of handstand during the turn (hips dipping down)
Arms bending during the turn
A half-turn that doesn't fully change direction
Not connecting smoothly into the next skill

quickTipsChecklist.description: Turn happens only when she's at full vertical
Hips don't dip during the turn
Full turn to face the other way
Flows into the next skill without a pause

videos.title: Pirouette on Bars — Stalding / Endo Turn

name: Gienger Release
description: Named after German gymnast Eberhard Gienger, this is a dramatic release element where your daughter swings forward, releases the bar, does a half-turn in the air, and re-catches the bar. It's similar to a Tkachev but with a twist involved. The result is she goes from swinging forward to flying backward and re-catching — a thrilling skill to watch.
whatToLookFor: A big, powerful swing into the release
A clean release of the bar with both hands
The half-turn happening cleanly in the air
Re-catching the bar with confidence — hesitation here is dangerous
Continuing to swing after the catch, not just dangling
commonMistakes: Not enough swing to get back to the bar after the half-turn
A fuzzy half-turn — not a full 180 degrees of rotation
Catching only one hand at a time
Body going loose in the air during the turn
Not resuming the swing after catching

quickTipsChecklist.description: Maximum swing before the release
Full 180° turn before catching
Re-catches bar with both hands simultaneously

videos.title: Gienger Release on Bars

name: Double Back Dismount
description: The double back dismount is one of the most exciting ways to finish a bars routine. Your daughter swings with maximum power, releases the bar, and performs two complete backflips in the air before landing. Judges and audiences love this dismount because of the obvious power and air time involved.
whatToLookFor: A huge swing to generate the power for two flips
A high release from the bar — she should fly up before going down
Two clearly separate flips — not a blur but two distinct rotations
A tight tuck (knees to chest) to maintain spin speed
Opening up from the tuck in time to stick the landing
commonMistakes: Not enough power in the swing, resulting in only getting halfway through the second flip
A loose tuck that slows down the spin mid-air
Opening from the tuck too late (crash) or too early (under-rotation)
Not going high enough off the bar
Landing off-balance and having to take multiple steps

quickTipsChecklist.description: Biggest possible swing before release
Goes up before going down
Knees to chest throughout both rotations
Body opens to land on feet, not on head

videos.title: Double Back Dismount — Gymnastics Bars

name: Pak Salto
description: A Pak salto is a beautiful transition from the high bar to the low bar. Your daughter swings forward on the high bar, releases it, does a backward pike salto in the air, and catches the low bar. It requires excellent timing and spatial awareness, and it's a staple connection in elite routines.
whatToLookFor: The swing on the high bar looking powerful and controlled
A clean release of the high bar
A tight pike position during the salto (legs straight, sharp fold at hips)
Re-catching the low bar cleanly with both hands
No arching or opening before catching the low bar
commonMistakes: Missing the low bar on the catch — timing is extremely precise here
A sloppy pike with bent legs
Opening too early from the pike before reaching the low bar
Catching with bent arms instead of reaching
A wobbly swing into the skill

quickTipsChecklist.description: Good swing on the high bar feeds the transition
Straight legs, sharp hip fold during the salto
Both hands re-catch the low bar at the same time

videos.title: Pak Salto — High Bar to Low Bar Transition

name: Shaposhnikova
description: Named after Soviet gymnast Natalia Shaposhnikova, this element is a transition from the low bar to the high bar. Your daughter swings on the low bar, releases it in a straddle position, and catches the high bar behind her. It's the opposite direction from most transitions and looks visually stunning.
whatToLookFor: A strong swing on the low bar with full momentum
A clear release and upward arc toward the high bar
The straddle position visible in the air
A confident catch of the high bar with both hands
Continuing to swing on the high bar after the catch
commonMistakes: Not generating enough swing to reach the high bar
Catching only one hand or missing the bar entirely
A narrow or bent-knee straddle
Body going loose and uncontrolled during the transition
Coming to a dead stop after catching instead of swinging through

quickTipsChecklist.description: Maximum swing before the transition begins
Legs spread clearly in the air
Both hands catch together, she keeps swinging

videos.title: Shaposhnikova — Elite Bars Transition

name: Clear Hip Circle to Handstand
description: Unlike the back hip circle where your daughter stays in contact with the bar, the clear hip circle requires her to circle the bar without her hips touching it — just her hands hold on. The skill ends in a handstand. It's much harder than a regular hip circle because there's no contact point to rely on.
whatToLookFor: Hips visibly clear of the bar throughout the circle
Maintaining a tight body shape throughout
Making it all the way up to handstand at the end
Controlled, fluid movement — not a stall in the middle
Straight arms throughout, especially at the top
commonMistakes: The hips touching the bar midway through (judges deduct for contact)
Slowing down midway because the body shape isn't held
Not making it all the way to handstand — stopping at 45 or 90 degrees
Bent arms, especially at the finish
A hollow body that becomes an arch partway through

quickTipsChecklist.description: No contact between hips and bar throughout
Stays compact and controlled throughout the circle
Completes all the way to vertical

videos.title: Clear Hip Circle to Handstand Tutorial

name: Nabieva Release
description: A spectacular and rare release element named after Azerbaijani gymnast Aliya Nabieva. Your daughter swings forward, releases the bar, does a full release into a full pirouette (twist) in the air, and re-catches the bar. Only a handful of gymnasts worldwide compete this skill, making it a true crowd-pleaser.
whatToLookFor: Maximum swing speed going into the release
Both hands releasing the bar simultaneously
A clear, complete twist in the air
Re-catching the bar — this is a very high-risk moment
Maintaining composure and continuing to swing after the catch
commonMistakes: Insufficient swing to have time for the twist AND make it back to the bar
An incomplete twist that leaves her catching at an odd angle
Catching the bar late or with only one hand
Body going loose mid-skill
Panicking mid-skill and losing the twist

quickTipsChecklist.description: Biggest swing possible before the release
360° turn clearly visible in the air
Catches bar cleanly and keeps swinging

videos.title: Nabieva Release — Elite Gymnastics Bars Tutorial

name: Deltchev
description: Named after Bulgarian gymnast Stoyan Deltchev, this is a release where your daughter swings forward, releases the bar, does a piked back salto (like a backflip with a pike fold), and re-catches the bar. It's similar to a Tkachev but with the legs straight and together in a pike instead of straddled.
whatToLookFor: A strong, high swing before the release
A tight pike (legs straight, sharp fold at hips)
Going over the bar in the pike position — she flies over it
Re-catching the bar cleanly after coming out of the pike
No bent knees during the pike phase
commonMistakes: A loose pike where legs are angled rather than sharply folded
Bent knees during the pike
Not going high enough over the bar to have time for the skill
Catching late with only one hand
Body going loose in the air

quickTipsChecklist.description: Strong swing to generate height over the bar
Sharp fold, no bending of the knees
Both hands catch bar simultaneously

videos.title: Deltchev Release — Advanced Gymnastics Bars Tutorial

name: Cartwheel on Beam
description: Doing a cartwheel on a 4-inch wide beam is completely different from a cartwheel on the floor. Your daughter must place each hand and foot exactly on the beam while upside-down and moving sideways. It requires precise coordination, strong arms, and a lot of courage. Every young gymnast works toward this milestone, and it's a huge confidence builder when they get it.
whatToLookFor: Straight arms — bent arms on the beam collapse the skill and can cause falls
Both hands placed one after the other on the center of the beam
A controlled, not rushed, rotation — fast is not always better on beam
Feet landing on the beam confidently, not scrambling for balance
Head staying neutral, not looking down in panic
commonMistakes: Looking down at the beam obsessively — judges notice the dropped chin
Bending the arms, which drops the shoulders toward the beam dangerously
A crooked cartwheel that puts hands and feet off-center on the beam
Rushing through the skill out of fear, losing control of each placement
Bent knees — legs should be straight and together at the top

quickTipsChecklist.description: Arms lock out throughout the cartwheel
Both hands placed squarely on the beam
Not rushed — each placement is deliberate
Feet land on beam with confidence, not scrambling

videos.title: Cartwheel on Beam — Gymneo Tutorial
Beam Cartwheel Progressions

name: Back Walkover on Beam
description: A back walkover on beam looks like a slow, graceful backwards cartwheel going over the top. Your daughter bends backward, places her hands on the beam, and walks her legs one at a time over her head. It requires a beautiful arch in the back, flexible shoulders, and the bravery to go backward on such a narrow surface.
whatToLookFor: A beautiful arch in the back as she goes over — the more flexible, the more stunning
Arms staying straight throughout — especially when her hands reach the beam
Legs splitting evenly as she walks over (a front split position at the top)
Controlled landing with one foot, then the other — not slamming feet down
Head gently leaning back, not tucked to chest
commonMistakes: Looking back with the head first, which causes the rest of the body to follow unpredictably
Stepping feet into the beam aggressively instead of placing them with control
Bent arms when hands hit the beam
Not arching the back enough — a straight body goes backward very differently than an arched one
Both feet landing at the same time instead of walking through

quickTipsChecklist.description: Back bends gracefully, not stiffly
Arms lock out when hands reach the beam
One foot, then the other — not both together
Feet land with precision, not urgency

videos.title: Back Walkover on Beam — Tutorial

name: Full Turn (Pirouette) on Beam
description: A full turn on beam means your daughter spins around 360 degrees while balanced on one foot, on a 4-inch wide beam. Unlike ballet, where the floor is your friend, a slightly off-center turn on beam can cause a fall. When done well, it looks effortlessly elegant. Judges look for control, balance, and a proper finishing position.
whatToLookFor: Rising up onto her toes (relevé) at the start of the turn
Arms coming in to help speed up the spin — like a figure skater pulling in
A clear 360-degree turn — not stopping at 270 or finishing sideways
The non-turning foot in a proper position (usually passé — knee out, toe to knee)
A controlled finish that shows she knows where she is
commonMistakes: Not rising high enough onto toes, which makes the turn wobbly
Arms staying wide instead of coming in to drive the spin
An incomplete turn that stops before 360 degrees
The working foot slipping down from passé mid-turn
Stepping off the beam on the dismount from the turn

quickTipsChecklist.description: Rises up tall onto toes before turning
Arms come toward body to help spin
Completes the full turn and knows where she stops
Non-turning foot stays in proper ballet position

videos.title: Full Turn on Beam Drills — Tammy Biggs

name: Back Handspring on Beam
description: A back handspring on the 4-inch beam is one of gymnastics' most challenging intermediate skills. Your daughter jumps backward, places both hands simultaneously on the beam while upside-down, then pushes off to land back on her feet. It requires absolute precision — the hands must land perfectly, and on beam, there's no margin for error.
whatToLookFor: Both hands landing on the beam at exactly the same time — any asymmetry causes a fall
Straight arms that immediately push off the beam
A tight, controlled body shape going over
Two feet landing simultaneously on the beam
No hesitation before the jump — commitment is essential
commonMistakes: One hand landing before the other, causing a sideways collapse
Peeking (looking for the beam while upside-down), which causes the body to rotate asymmetrically
A weak jump that doesn't provide enough power to get over
Landing with feet apart instead of together
Bending the arms on the beam instead of immediately pushing off

quickTipsChecklist.description: Both hands hit beam at exact same moment
Head stays neutral, doesn't look for beam mid-skill
Arms push immediately after landing
Both feet land together, not one at a time

videos.title: Back Handspring Step-by-Step Tutorial

name: Round-off Dismount
description: A round-off dismount means your daughter does a round-off from the beam and lands on the mat below. It's a half-cartwheel that finishes with both feet together, launching her off the end of the beam. This is often one of the first real dismounts gymnasts learn, and when done well, it looks clean and decisive.
whatToLookFor: A confident approach to the end of the beam
Straight arms during the handstand phase
Both feet snapping together at the top of the round-off
A clean, decisive push off the beam — not just falling off
A solid landing with bent knees that absorbs the impact
commonMistakes: A crooked round-off that sends her sideways off the beam instead of down
Feet not coming together at the top — this creates an uneven, ugly landing
Not pushing hard enough, resulting in a short, low flight phase
Landing stiff-legged, which is hard on the joints and takes away score
Looking down on the approach, throwing off her spatial awareness

quickTipsChecklist.description: Arms straight, body tight in the handstand phase
Both feet join together at the top
Active push creates good height and distance
Lands softly with knees bent, not stiff

videos.title: Round-off Beam Dismount Tutorial

name: Back Layout Salto (Beam)
description: A back salto (backflip) on beam in layout position — body completely straight, no tuck or pike — is one of the most visually striking skills in women's gymnastics. Your daughter takes off from both feet, flips all the way backward with a straight body, and lands back on the same 4-inch beam. The difficulty of landing back on the beam after a full flip is enormous.
whatToLookFor: A strong jump upward at takeoff — she should go UP before going OVER
A completely straight, stretched body during the flip (no bend at hips or knees)
Good height — you should see daylight under her feet at the peak
A confident, two-foot landing back on the beam
Controlled arms that help with timing and balance on landing
commonMistakes: A piked or tucked body instead of layout — bent hips or knees reduce the score significantly
Not getting enough height, which causes a rushed, under-rotated flip
Landing with feet apart on the beam — they must land together
Over-rotating and losing her footing
An arched back (not the same as layout — arching is a mistake)

quickTipsChecklist.description: Strong upward jump, height before rotation
No bend anywhere from shoulders to toes
Clear rise before the flip happens
Both feet land together back on the beam

videos.title: Back Layout on Beam — Tutorial

name: Wolf Turn on Beam
description: A wolf turn is a spin on one foot in a crouching position — one leg is squatting while the other is extended out to the side. It looks like a dancer in a crouching arabesque. This turn became enormously popular after top gymnasts started doing triple and quadruple wolf turns for maximum difficulty value.
whatToLookFor: The squatting leg at roughly a 90-degree angle (not standing tall)
The extended leg held straight out to the side
Spinning smoothly on the toes or flat foot of the squatting leg
Completing the full number of rotations clearly
A controlled finish — not falling over at the end of the spin
commonMistakes: Rising up too tall and turning it into a regular turn instead of a wolf shape
The extended leg bending at the knee — it should stay straight
Losing balance and stepping off the beam mid-spin
An incomplete turn — the number of rotations must be clear and intentional
The working foot slipping during the turn

quickTipsChecklist.description: Squatting leg at 90°, not standing
The out leg stays completely straight
Every full turn is visible and complete
Stops without wobbling or stepping off

videos.title: Wolf Turn on Beam Tutorial

name: Switch Ring Leap
description: This is one of the most beautiful leaps in gymnastics. Your daughter takes off from one foot, does a switch of the legs in the air (the front leg swings back and the back leg swings forward), then bends the back knee and grabs or reaches for the foot behind her — making a 'ring' shape with her body. The combination of height, flexibility, and control makes this highly valued.
whatToLookFor: A clear split in the air before the switch happens
The switch of legs happening visibly — front becomes back and back becomes front
The back leg bending to form the ring shape — her foot should ideally reach toward her head
Remaining on the beam — this is extra impressive because of how much is happening
Good height so there's time for the switch and the ring shape to form
commonMistakes: The switch not happening — it looks like just a regular split leap
The back leg not bending enough to form the ring shape — a shallow ring scores lower
Not enough height, causing a rushed switch
Landing off-balance after the complex movement
Flexibility limiting how much of the ring she can achieve

quickTipsChecklist.description: Legs clearly switch positions in the air
Heel comes toward head to form ring shape
Enough air time for all the movement to happen
Controlled landing back on the 4-inch surface

videos.title: Switch Ring Leap on Beam

name: Sheep Jump
description: A sheep jump looks exactly like its name — your daughter jumps with both feet and bends backward sharply while in the air, with both legs kicking up behind her and her head dropping back. The shape it makes in the air is like a U or a backwards arch. It requires strong back flexibility and good air awareness.
whatToLookFor: Both legs kicking up behind her simultaneously — not one at a time
Her head dropping backward to deepen the arch
The knees bending to bring the feet toward the head
Good height — the more air time, the better the shape looks
A controlled, two-foot landing back on the beam
commonMistakes: Only one leg kicking up — creates an asymmetrical, confused-looking skill
Not arching the back enough — a flat sheep jump misses the point of the skill
Not getting enough height to form the shape cleanly
Landing with feet apart or stumbling on landing
The head not dropping back, keeping the body stiff

quickTipsChecklist.description: Simultaneous kick of both heels toward head
Head arches back to deepen the U-shape
Jumps up enough to form the shape clearly

videos.title: Sheep Jump on Beam Tutorial

name: Aerial Cartwheel on Beam (Side Aerial)
description: The side aerial (also called aerial cartwheel) is one of the most breathtaking beam skills you will ever watch: your daughter does a full sideways cartwheel over the beam without her hands ever touching it. She launches off one foot, flies sideways through the air in a cartwheel-like arc, and lands back on the beam on two feet — all without touching her hands down. The crowd always reacts to this skill. It requires extraordinary courage and is a big step up from a regular cartwheel on beam.
whatToLookFor: A powerful push off the takeoff leg — she needs serious power to clear the beam without hands
A high, arching flight path — you should see her body rise well above the beam
A straight body in a clear cartwheel shape in the air (not bent or tucked)
Both hands staying at her sides — any hand touch on the beam is scored as a fall
A two-foot landing centered on the beam, not falling off to either side
commonMistakes: Touching hands to the beam in panic — this is counted as a fall and is a major deduction
Not enough height, causing the hips to brush the beam on the way over
Over-rotating or under-rotating, causing a sideways landing off the beam
A bent body shape in the air that loses the clean cartwheel line

quickTipsChecklist.description: Strong push-off creates the height needed to clear the beam
Hands never touch the beam — any touch is a fall
Her body makes a visible sideways arc over the beam
Both feet land on the beam at the same time, centered

videos.title: Aerial Cartwheel on Beam — Tutorial

name: Standing Back Tuck (Beam Dismount)
description: A standing back tuck dismount means your daughter jumps backward from the end of the beam, tucks her knees to her chest for a backflip, and lands on the mat below. This is a clean, impressive dismount that shows her backflip skills. The 'standing' part means she takes off from two feet — no run-up.
whatToLookFor: A strong two-foot jump upward from the beam
A tight tuck with knees pulled firmly to the chest
The flip completing before her feet hit the mat
Arms controlling the rotation (coming in to speed up, then opening to slow down)
A solid landing with knees bent
commonMistakes: Not jumping high enough — the height gives her time to flip fully
A loose tuck where knees aren't pulled to chest (slows the rotation)
Throwing the head back first instead of jumping up first (causes over-rotation)
Not opening from the tuck in time and landing in a ball
Stepping sideways on landing

quickTipsChecklist.description: Two-foot jump upward before any flip motion
Knees to chest, not a slow open tuck
Feet drop to mat in time for a solid landing

videos.title: Back Tuck Dismount from Beam

name: Double Pike Dismount (Beam)
description: An elite-level dismount where your daughter performs two complete backflips from the beam in a pike position (legs straight, sharp hip fold). The two flips happen in rapid succession, making it look like a blur of controlled rotation. It's one of the most impressive ways to end a beam routine.
whatToLookFor: Explosive jump upward from the beam at takeoff
A tight pike position — straight legs, sharp fold at the hips
Two clearly distinct flips visible (not a blur of undefined rotation)
Opening from the pike in time to land on feet
Ideally sticking the landing — elite gymnasts aim for no steps
commonMistakes: Not generating enough power to complete two full rotations
Loose pike — bent knees inside the pike reduce score
Only completing 1.5 rotations and landing off-axis
Over-rotating and crashing forward on landing
Opening too early and under-rotating

quickTipsChecklist.description: Biggest possible jump to start the dismount
Clean pike position with fully straight legs
Both flips clearly distinguishable
Opens in time for a two-foot landing

videos.title: Double Pike Dismount — Beam

name: Acrobatic Series (Beam)
description: A beam series is when your daughter performs two or more acrobatic skills in a row without stopping — for example, a back handspring followed immediately by a back layout. Connecting skills gives bonus points. It's like a tumbling pass on the floor, but on a 4-inch beam. The timing and momentum control required is extraordinary.
whatToLookFor: A smooth, flowing connection between the skills — no pause or wobble in the middle
Maintaining the quality of each individual skill within the series
Good landing position from the first skill that naturally feeds into the next
Confidence throughout — hesitation breaks the connection
Both skills executed at their full quality, not just surviving to the next one
commonMistakes: A visible pause or balance check between the skills (this removes the connection bonus)
The second skill looking weaker than the first — fatigue and nerves set in
Landing the first skill off-center on the beam, making the second skill dangerous
A shortened or rushed second skill because momentum carried incorrectly
Over-rotating the first skill and running out of beam

quickTipsChecklist.description: Seamless connection — one flows directly into the next
Second skill looks as good as the first
No hesitation or balance check mid-series

videos.title: Acrobatic Series on Beam — Connection Skills

name: Round-off Back Handspring
description: This is one of the foundational tumbling combinations in gymnastics — the starting point for almost every floor tumbling pass. The round-off is a half-cartwheel that flips your daughter backward, and the back handspring uses that backward momentum to do a backwards hand-stand spring. Together, they look like a fast, flowing backwards sequence. This combination builds the power for back flips and more complex tumbling.
whatToLookFor: A fast, energetic round-off that generates backwards speed
The feet snapping together at the end of the round-off
Immediately using that momentum to launch into the back handspring
Both hands hitting the floor at the same time in the back handspring
Straight arms throughout both skills
commonMistakes: A slow round-off that kills the momentum for the back handspring
Feet not snapping together at the top of the round-off
Pausing between the round-off and back handspring — losing all momentum
One hand landing before the other in the back handspring
Arching the back in the back handspring instead of staying hollow

quickTipsChecklist.description: Round-off builds backwards speed — it should feel fast
At the top of the round-off, feet join
Back handspring starts immediately after round-off
Arms lock out throughout both elements

videos.title: Round-Off Back Handspring to Layout — Coach Meggin

name: Back Tuck (Backflip)
description: A back tuck — commonly called a backflip — is when your daughter jumps backward and does a complete backwards flip with her knees tucked to her chest. It's often the first major trick gymnasts learn, and achieving it is one of the biggest milestones in youth gymnastics. When done from a round-off or back handspring, it's a powerful, exciting skill.
whatToLookFor: A strong, upward jump at takeoff — she should go UP, not just backward
Knees pulled tightly to the chest in the tuck position
The rotation completing before she reaches the mat
Arms helping control the spin — in tight when flipping, out when landing
A landing with feet together and knees bent
commonMistakes: Throwing the head backward before jumping up — this causes over-rotation and can be dangerous
A loose, open tuck where knees aren't pulled in — slows the rotation
Jumping backward (horizontally) instead of up — reduces the flip time
Landing with the back curved and head forward instead of standing tall
One foot landing before the other

quickTipsChecklist.description: Go upward before going backward
Knees pulled firmly to chest
Arms tighten to speed up, open to slow down
Chest comes up, not hunched forward

videos.title: Back Tuck (Backflip) Tutorial for Beginners

name: Back Layout
description: A back layout is a backflip with the body completely straight — like a plank rotating through the air. It's harder than a back tuck because straight bodies rotate more slowly, so your daughter needs to generate more power. When done well, it's one of the most beautiful skills in gymnastics — watching someone fly through the air in a perfect straight line is stunning.
whatToLookFor: The body looking completely straight from head to toe — no bend at hips or knees
A slightly hollow body (not arched) — judges distinguish between these
Excellent height — the power needed for a layout shows
A tight, controlled body throughout the entire flip
Arms helping drive the rotation
commonMistakes: Piking at the hips (bending forward at the waist) — this is the most common layout error
Arching the back — this is different from layout and loses points
Not getting enough height because the gymnast isn't generating enough power
Head and eyes dropping down mid-skill
Coming down before completing the full rotation

quickTipsChecklist.description: No hip or knee bend — one solid straight line
Slightly hollow core, not banana-shaped back
Significant rise visible above the floor
Tight body from takeoff to landing

videos.title: Back Layout Gymnastics Floor — Tutorial

name: Full Twist (Back Layout Full)
description: A 'full' (or full twist) is when your daughter does a back layout with a complete 360-degree twist added. She flips backward AND spins around her vertical axis at the same time. It's two forms of rotation happening simultaneously. This is one of the most common intermediate skills in women's gymnastics and looks spectacular when done well.
whatToLookFor: The same strong, powerful entry as the layout (round-off, back handspring, then full)
A straight body throughout the twist — no pike or arch
One complete, clean 360-degree twist — not a fuzzy 270 or 300 degrees
Arms staying tight during the twist, then opening to control landing
Landing with chest up, not hunched forward
commonMistakes: An incomplete twist — stopping at 270 degrees and landing sideways
Piking to start the twist instead of keeping the body straight
Twisting too late (after the flip is mostly done) instead of simultaneously
Arms going wide during the twist, which slows the rotation
Over-rotating and crashing forward

quickTipsChecklist.description: No pike or arch while twisting
Complete single twist — visibly finishes fully
Arms stay in to maintain twist speed

quickTipsChecklist.cue: Lands facing same direction as takeoff
quickTipsChecklist.description: Full = back to facing the same direction

videos.title: Full Twist (Layout Full) — Gymnastics Floor Tutorial

name: Double Back Tuck (Floor)
description: A double back tuck is two complete backflips in a row in a single jump — one of the most exciting skills on floor. Your daughter needs enormous power to generate two complete rotations. Watching a gymnast fly up, flip twice, and land on her feet is one of gymnastics' most crowd-pleasing moments.
whatToLookFor: A very powerful approach — the round-off and back handspring must be fast
An explosive jump upward at the takeoff
Two clearly distinct tucks visible — the second flip should be as controlled as the first
Opening from the tuck in time to see the mat and prepare for landing
A solid landing — sticking it is the goal
commonMistakes: Not having enough power — only completing 1.5 flips and crashing
A loose tuck that slows the rotation midway through the second flip
Opening too early from the first flip and not having enough spin for the second
Landing off-axis, stumbling sideways
Losing height (the whole pass going too low rather than up)

quickTipsChecklist.description: Fast round-off, fast back handspring going in
Highest possible jump at takeoff
Knees to chest for both rotations
Spots the landing and opens in time

videos.title: Double Back Tuck — Floor Exercise

name: Double Pike (Floor)
description: Two complete backflips with a straight-leg pike (sharp bend at hips, legs fully extended) — even harder than a double tuck because the pike position rotates more slowly. Your daughter must generate enough power to make two full rotations while maintaining perfect pike form. This is a staple skill at elite levels.
whatToLookFor: An even more powerful approach than a double tuck
A sharp, tight pike — legs fully straight, hips folded severely
Two full rotations completing before the landing
Opening from the pike in time to see the floor
Chest coming up on landing, not hunching forward
commonMistakes: A soft pike with bent knees — this is heavily penalized because it changes the skill
Under-rotating due to the slower rotation speed of a pike
Not getting enough height to complete two rotations in pike
Opening too early and finishing on the wrong foot
Over-rotating and landing on the chest

quickTipsChecklist.description: Even faster approach than double tuck
Strict pike with fully straight legs
Both flips complete before landing
Body opens from pike before landing

videos.title: Double Pike — Floor Exercise Gymnastics

name: Triple Double (Biles II)
technicalName: Triple-Double / Double Salto with Triple Twist
description: The Biles II is the hardest skill in women's gymnastics — in the world. Named after Simone Biles, it involves two backflips with three twists simultaneously. No one else in competitive gymnastics has done this skill. When Simone performs it, it's one of those moments where the entire arena falls silent and then erupts in disbelief. This skill has its own letter value that didn't exist in the Code of Points before Biles.
whatToLookFor: This skill is so rare you will only see Simone Biles do it — any video of it is worth showing
Two complete backflips AND three complete twists happening at the same time
An almost incomprehensible amount of rotation packed into a single skill
The landing happening on the spring floor after all that spinning
Pure power, speed, and precision throughout
commonMistakes: This skill has no common mistakes in the normal sense — very few people can attempt it
If your daughter is learning this, her coach has things well in hand

quickTipsChecklist.description: Maximum power, maximum height, maximum body tension

videos.title: Biles — Triple Double at World Championships

name: Split Leap
description: A split leap is when your daughter takes off from one foot and reaches a full split (180-degree leg split) in the air before landing. It's a dance element required in every level of gymnastics. A beautiful split leap looks like your daughter is flying — back leg parallel to the floor, front leg reaching forward, body upright and elegant.
whatToLookFor: A 180-degree split — both legs fully extended and level with each other
Good height — she should visibly leave the ground
An upright torso — not leaning forward over the front leg
Pointed toes on both feet throughout the leap
A graceful, confident landing on the front leg
commonMistakes: A 'bent' split where the back leg hasn't kicked up high enough — judges measure the angle
Leaning forward with the chest over the front leg
Flexed (bent-back) feet instead of pointed toes — this is a clear deduction
Not getting enough height, making the split hard to achieve in the air
Landing heavily with no grace or control

quickTipsChecklist.description: Both legs fully extended and level in the air
Chest doesn't lean forward over the front leg
Both feet have beautiful pointed toes
Visibly leaves the ground with the leap

videos.title: Split Leap Drill — How to Improve Your Split Leap

name: Switch Leap
description: A switch leap starts like a split leap but adds a leg switch in the air — your daughter takes off with the right leg forward, then switches so the left leg is forward when she lands. It requires more air time and coordination than a basic split leap and demonstrates that your daughter has good spatial awareness and flexibility.
whatToLookFor: A clear switch of the legs in the air — the leading leg changes
A full split position achieved after the switch (not just after takeoff)
Good height to have time for the switch to occur
Pointed toes on both feet
Landing on the correct foot (the one that came forward after the switch)
commonMistakes: The switch not happening — it just looks like a regular split leap
The split only happening before the switch, not after (wrong timing)
Not enough height for the switch to occur cleanly
One or both feet flexed (not pointed) during the leap
Landing off-balance after the switch

quickTipsChecklist.description: Legs clearly change front-to-back in the air
Full split achieved after the leg change
Enough height for the switch to happen cleanly
Feet stay pointed throughout

videos.title: Switch Leap Gymnastics Tutorial

name: Fouetté Turn
description: Named from ballet, a fouetté turn involves whipping the leg around to generate spin — your daughter kicks her working leg out to the side and then brings it in repeatedly to create or maintain rotation. Multiple fouetté turns in a row (like a chain of turns) are called 'fouetté series' and show advanced dance skill.
whatToLookFor: The whipping leg motion that drives each turn
Rising up on the supporting leg's toes for the spin
A consistent spot (focus point) — she should snap her head around with each turn
Maintaining the same height and position with each turn in a series
A controlled finish that ends cleanly
commonMistakes: Not spotting — the head should snap around sharply to a single focus point each turn
Sinking down on the supporting leg instead of staying on high toes
The whipping leg going too high or not high enough
Traveling across the floor instead of turning in place
The series getting worse as it goes — the last turn should be as good as the first

quickTipsChecklist.description: Head snaps to focus point with each rotation
Stays high on toes throughout the series
Same leg motion drives every turn

videos.title: Fouetté Turns — Gymnastics Floor

name: Straddle Jump
description: A straddle jump is when your daughter jumps straight up and spreads her legs out to the sides in the air, reaching for her toes. It's a staple of any floor routine and is one of the first jumps gymnasts learn. The wider the spread and the more she can reach toward her feet, the better the score.
whatToLookFor: A high jump — she should clearly leave the ground
Legs spreading wide out to the sides (the wider the better)
Arms reaching toward the toes
Pointed toes on both feet
Two-foot landing with control (no stumbling or extra steps)
commonMistakes: A small jump that doesn't give enough air time for the straddle
Legs not going wide enough — a narrow straddle looks weak
Feet flexed (toes not pointed) — an automatic deduction
Not reaching toward the feet — arms hanging down at the sides
Landing on one foot or stumbling forward

quickTipsChecklist.description: Jumps as high as possible
Legs spread far out to the sides
Arms stretch toward feet
Both feet pointed throughout the jump

videos.title: Straddle Jump Tutorial — Gymnastics Floor

name: Double Layout (Floor)
description: Two backflips in a row with a completely straight body position — the hardest commonly competed tumbling pass in elite women's gymnastics (outside the Biles family of skills). The straight body position rotates more slowly than tucked or piked, so your daughter must generate incredible power and height. Simone Biles and very few others compete this skill regularly.
whatToLookFor: A spectacularly powerful approach — the fastest, most explosive run of her tumbling
A completely straight body for BOTH flips — no bend anywhere
Remarkable height — you should see her well above the floor at the peak
Two distinct flips clearly visible
A landing that shows she's in control after all that rotation
commonMistakes: Not generating enough power — attempting this without adequate strength and training is dangerous
Piking during the second flip — the layout must be maintained for both
Insufficient height, causing the second flip to be completed very close to the ground
Over-rotating and crashing forward
Only completing 1.5 layouts

quickTipsChecklist.description: Absolute maximum speed and power into the tumbling
Perfect layout maintained for both rotations
Rises far above the floor
Both rotations clearly distinguishable

videos.title: Double Layout — Elite Floor Exercise

name: Wolf Turn (Floor)
description: The floor wolf turn is the same skill as on beam but on the spring floor — your daughter squats on one leg with the other extended out to the side and spins. The spring floor makes balancing easier than beam but the turn still requires flexibility, balance, and the ability to spot quickly. Multiple wolf turns in a row show high difficulty.
whatToLookFor: A clear squat position — the bent leg should be at 90 degrees
The extended leg held straight throughout
A smooth, consistent spin with good speed
The number of rotations matching what was attempted (1, 2, or more turns)
A clean finish without stumbling or stepping
commonMistakes: Rising up too high — should be in a low wolf position, not standing
The extended leg bending at the knee
Stopping before completing all rotations
Traveling across the floor while turning (should spin in place)
Losing balance at the end

quickTipsChecklist.description: Squatting leg at 90°, low position
Side leg stays fully extended
Every rotation visibly completes

videos.title: Wolf Turn Floor Exercise Tutorial

name: Squat On, Jump Off
description: This is often the very first real vault a young gymnast learns — usually in Level 1-3 or XCEL Bronze. She runs down the runway, bounces off the springboard, and lands both feet on top of the vault table in a squat (crouching) position. From there, she stands up and jumps off to land on the mat. Think of it like jumping on and then off a big box.
whatToLookFor: A confident run and a good two-foot jump onto the springboard
Both feet landing squarely on top of the vault table at the same time
A brief moment of balance in the squat position before jumping off
Arms reaching forward as she jumps off the table
A controlled landing with bent knees — not a stumble forward
commonMistakes: Landing only one foot on the vault table instead of two simultaneously
Squatting too far back on the table and falling toward the springboard side
Jumping off the table before finding balance, which leads to a forward crash
Not using enough spring off the board, making the feet barely reach the table

quickTipsChecklist.description: Both feet land on the vault top at the same moment
She pauses a moment in squat before jumping off
Arms reach out in front as she jumps off the table
Bent knees absorb the landing — no crashing or stumbling

videos.title: Squat On Jump Off Vault — Beginner Gymnastics

name: Front Handspring Vault with Full Twist
description: This vault starts exactly like the basic front handspring vault — she runs, hits the springboard, and pushes off the table — but then adds a full 360-degree twist while in the air before landing. Her body spins like a corkscrew during the flight phase. It's a crowd-pleasing skill because the spin happens while she's airborne, making it look much more impressive than the basic version.
whatToLookFor: A powerful, fast run that doesn't slow down near the board
Strong straight arms on the table — same foundation as the basic FHS
A clean, controlled twist that starts immediately after leaving the table
The twist completing fully before she begins to land (not still spinning on the mat)
A stable landing — the twist makes balance harder, so extra steps are common but penalized
commonMistakes: Starting the twist too early (on the table) instead of in the air — looks messy and loses power
An incomplete twist where she lands at only 270 degrees, facing sideways
Losing height by twisting too quickly — the vault should still show a clear flight phase
Landing off-axis and stumbling sideways because the twist wasn't straight

quickTipsChecklist.description: Full sprint all the way to the board
The rotation begins after she leaves the table, not on it
She lands facing the vault — twist is complete before landing
Feet together, knees bent, minimal steps

videos.title: Front Handspring Full Twist Vault Tutorial

name: Mill Circle
description: The mill circle is one of the first bar skills young gymnasts learn — it's a fundamental Level 3 skill done on the low bar. Starting in a straddle position with her hips resting on the bar (one leg in front, one behind), she leans forward and swings all the way around the bar in a full circle, ending back where she started. It looks like she's rotating around a horizontal pole with her hips as the pivot point.
whatToLookFor: A clear straddle start — one leg in front, one behind, hips on the bar
A confident lean forward to initiate the circle — she shouldn't hesitate
Her body staying close to the bar throughout the rotation
Straight legs during the circle (bent knees are a common deduction)
A clean finish back in the straddle position, in control
commonMistakes: Bending the knees during the circle, which loses the clean line judges look for
Sliding off the bar during the rotation — usually because hips aren't centered
Not leaning forward enough to start the circle, causing the skill to stall halfway
Letting go of the bar in a panic mid-circle — this is a safety concern

quickTipsChecklist.description: One leg forward, one behind, hips resting on the bar
Leans into the rotation without stopping
Knees stay extended throughout the circle
Completes the circle and returns to start position

videos.title: Mill Circle on Bars — Level 3 Gymnastics Tutorial

name: Flyaway Dismount
description: The flyaway is the most common bars dismount you'll see at gymnastics meets from Level 4 all the way up. At the peak of a giant swing or underswing, your daughter releases the bar and flies through the air in a straight (layout) or tucked position, landing on the mat facing away from the bar. The name says it all — she literally flies away from the bar.
whatToLookFor: A powerful giant swing or underswing leading into the dismount
A clean release of both hands at the same moment — no one hand releasing early
A straight or tight body position in the air (not sagging or arching randomly)
Good height after release — she should visibly rise before coming down
Landing on two feet with control — not pitching forward onto hands
commonMistakes: Releasing the bar too early (at the bottom of the swing) instead of at the top, which kills height
Letting go with one hand before the other, causing a sideways twist
A bent/piked body during the flight that makes the dismount look uncontrolled
Over-rotating and landing facing the bar instead of away from it
A blind landing — she should be spotting the mat before her feet arrive

quickTipsChecklist.description: A big swinging motion powers the release
Simultaneous release for a straight flight
She rises clearly before coming down to the mat
Sticks on two feet facing away from the bar

videos.title: Flyaway Dismount Gymnastics Bars Tutorial

name: Stalder Circle
description: Named after Swiss gymnast Josef Stalder, this is a circling skill where your daughter keeps her legs in a straddle (wide-apart) position while circling the bar. Instead of a giant swing where legs stay together, the Stalder requires her to hold a pike-straddle shape with legs pressed against the bar on each side as she rotates. It demands extreme flexibility and body tension — making it a big step up from a regular giant swing.
whatToLookFor: A clear straddle shape entering the circle — legs noticeably wide apart
Her legs pressing close to the bar (not floating away) as she goes around
A tight body position maintained throughout — no loosening mid-rotation
A smooth, continuous rotation rather than a jerky or stalled circle
A clean finish into a handstand or cast position after completing the circle
commonMistakes: Legs drifting away from the bar during the circle, losing the required shape
A hip angle that opens too early, killing the rotation before completion
Insufficient flexibility — the straddle requires exceptional hip flexibility
Stalling at the back or top of the circle and losing momentum

quickTipsChecklist.description: Legs stay wide apart throughout the entire circle
Thighs press near the bar as she rotates around
Smooth continuous rotation with no hesitation
Arrives back at handstand or cast in control

videos.title: Stalder Circle Gymnastics Uneven Bars

name: Forward Roll on Beam
description: A forward somersault performed entirely on the 4-inch wide balance beam. Your daughter tucks her chin, places her hands on the beam, and rolls forward, bringing her feet over and around to land back on the beam in a standing position. It's the same basic motion as a floor forward roll — but done on a beam barely wider than a textbook. Level 3 gymnasts learn this as one of their first beam acrobatic elements.
whatToLookFor: Chin tucked to chest before initiating the roll — this protects her neck
Hands placed flat and evenly on the beam, shoulder-width apart
A smooth, controlled roll along the spine — not a crash or a topple to the side
Feet landing squarely on the beam when she comes around — not too wide or narrow
Standing up in control without grabbing the beam with hands
commonMistakes: Not tucking the chin, causing her to roll on her head instead of the back of her skull
Placing hands unevenly, which starts the roll with a sideways lean
Rolling too fast and losing control when her feet hit the beam
Overbalancing forward onto her face when standing up from the roll

quickTipsChecklist.description: Chin goes to chest first — protects her neck
Both hands on the beam, centered, at the same time
Smooth rotation — not too fast, not stalled
Feet arrive squarely on the beam when coming up to stand

videos.title: Forward Roll on Balance Beam — Gymnastics Tutorial

name: Arabesque / Scale on Beam
description: An arabesque is a classical ballet position held on the beam: your daughter balances on one leg while extending the other leg straight behind her, with her arms gracefully extended. A scale is similar but the back leg is raised very high (ideally at or above hip height). These are dance elements required in almost every beam routine at every level. They look elegant but require strong balance and flexibility.
whatToLookFor: Standing leg straight and not wobbling — the base of the whole position
Back leg lifted high and straight — ideally at or above hip level
Hips level (not tilted) — a common deduction judges look for
Arms graceful and extended, not tensed or flailing for balance
A still, held position — judges want to see she can maintain it, not just pass through it
commonMistakes: A bent knee on the standing leg, which makes the whole position look weak
Hips tilting sideways as the back leg rises — this is a significant deduction
Back leg barely at hip height when it should be higher
Arms grabbing for balance rather than held in a controlled shape
Wobbling or stepping out before the hold time is complete

quickTipsChecklist.description: The support leg is fully extended, not bent
The raised leg is at or above hip height
Both hips are at the same height — no tilting
Arms are held in a beautiful shape, not flailing

videos.title: Arabesque on Balance Beam — Gymnastics Dance Elements

name: Split on Beam
description: Performing a full split on the balance beam means lowering both legs into a flat split position — front to back — along a 4-inch wide surface. Your daughter sinks from a standing or kneeling position down until both legs are fully extended along the beam. It's one of the most visually striking held positions in gymnastics and requires the same flexibility as a floor split, plus the nerve to do it at beam height.
whatToLookFor: Hips completely square and facing forward — not turned out to one side to cheat the split
Both legs fully extended and flat on the beam — not hovering above it
Front toes pointed — no flexed foot on the leading leg
Back toes pointed and leg flat against the beam
A controlled lowering down and controlled rising back up — no plopping or grabbing
commonMistakes: Hips rotating sideways to make the split look fuller than it actually is — judges see this easily
Front foot flexed (toes up) instead of pointed, which is a deduction
Not achieving full contact between both legs and the beam — still a few inches off
Grabbing the beam with hands when lowering or rising, causing an additional deduction

quickTipsChecklist.description: Hips face directly forward, not rotated to the side
Full contact from hip to toe on both sides
Both feet pointed throughout the skill
Rises back up without using hands for support

videos.title: Split on Balance Beam — Gymnastics Tutorial

name: Sissone on Beam
description: A sissone is a ballet-derived jump on the beam where your daughter takes off from two feet and lands on one foot, with the other leg extended to the side or rear. It creates a brief split shape in the air. On the beam, the sissone is often used as a connecting element in jump series — two or three jumps linked together — which scores bonus points. Parents often see this as a quick small jump that transitions into a bigger leap.
whatToLookFor: A clear two-foot takeoff — both feet leave the beam at the same moment
A split shape in the air — legs separating visibly during the jump
A one-foot landing in balance — her free leg stays extended rather than dropping immediately
Pointed toes throughout the jump, both in the air and on landing
The landing being controlled — she shouldn't wobble or put the free foot down for balance
commonMistakes: A weak jump height — low jumps mean judges can't see the split shape clearly
Both feet landing instead of just one (turns it into a different, lower-value skill)
The free leg immediately dropping on landing instead of staying extended
Flexed feet (toes up) which are always penalized on beam dance elements

quickTipsChecklist.description: Both feet push off the beam simultaneously
Legs clearly separate during the jump
Lands on one foot with the other leg extended
Toes pointed from takeoff through landing

videos.title: Sissone on Balance Beam — Gymnastics Jump Series

name: Back Walkover
description: The back walkover is one of the most common skills you'll see young gymnasts practice — it's usually one of the first real acrobatic skills a Level 3 gymnast learns. Starting from a stand, your daughter arches backward, plants one hand then the other on the floor, kicks her legs over one at a time, and stands back up. Think of it as a bridge that keeps moving. It requires a good backbend (bridge) and the confidence to kick over backwards.
whatToLookFor: A deep, fluid arch as she reaches back — not a sudden jerk backward
Her eyes finding the floor behind her before her hands land (she should see where she's going)
Hands landing sequentially — one at a time, not both at once
Legs splitting wide as they pass over (the wider the better — more flexibility, better score)
Standing up all the way with arms overhead, not hunching forward on the finish
commonMistakes: Closing her eyes during the backbend — she needs to see the floor so her hands know where to go
Bending her knees as the legs pass over, which collapses the clean line judges want to see
Both hands landing simultaneously instead of sequentially (makes it look like a different, messier skill)
Not finishing fully upright — stepping forward in a crouch instead of stretching tall

quickTipsChecklist.description: She looks back and sees the mat before her hands land
Sequential hand placement, not a simultaneous plop
Knees don't bend as legs pass overhead
Stands up fully with arms overhead, not hunching

videos.title: Back Walkover Tutorial — Gymnastics for Beginners

name: Front Walkover
description: The front walkover is the forward-facing companion to the back walkover — equally common at Level 3 and above. Your daughter kicks one leg up overhead, bridges forward, and walks her hands down while her legs pass over in a split, landing one foot at a time to stand up. It flows continuously forward and requires both flexibility (a good split position overhead) and strength (to push up from the bridge to standing). Judges love a big, split-legged version.
whatToLookFor: A high kick to start — the lead leg should go up aggressively to power the skill
Straight arms when hands contact the floor — bent arms collapse the bridge
A clear split overhead — legs wide apart when directly above her (this is the most visible part)
Landing one foot at a time, not both together (landing both together is the front handspring — a different skill)
Standing up fully at the finish with arms raised, not bent-over
commonMistakes: A weak kick that doesn't generate enough momentum to carry her over (she stalls in the bridge)
Bent elbows when hands hit the floor, which can be an injury risk and is penalized
A poor split overhead — legs close together looks messy and reduces the score
Both feet landing at the same time — this changes it to a front handspring (different skill entirely)

quickTipsChecklist.description: Lead leg kicks up high to power the skill over
Elbows locked, not bent, when hands touch
Legs split wide when directly overhead
Steps down one foot then the other — not both together

videos.title: Front Walkover Tutorial — Gymnastics

name: Front Handspring
description: The front handspring is like a supercharged front walkover — instead of stepping down one foot at a time, she snaps both feet down together with power, landing on two feet simultaneously. She runs, hurdles (a one-two step), throws her arms to the floor, kicks her legs overhead, and snaps down to an upright landing. It's often the first punch tumbling skill a gymnast learns, because it teaches the powerful snap-down action needed for all future front passes.
whatToLookFor: A confident hurdle step going into the skill — this generates the power
Straight arms the moment hands contact the floor — this is non-negotiable
A tight body with legs together as they pass over (not split apart like a walkover)
A sharp snap-down — both feet hitting the floor together with a visible snap of the body
Standing upright on the landing — not crashing forward onto bent knees
commonMistakes: Bending arms when hands hit the floor — this kills the power and risks the shoulders
Legs split apart instead of together during the flight (makes it look like a front walkover instead)
A slow, soft landing instead of a sharp snap-down — she should pop up not dribble down
Not following through fully: landing in a deep crouch instead of an upright stand

quickTipsChecklist.description: Arms fully locked the instant hands contact the mat
Legs stay together as they pass overhead — not split
Both feet hit the floor together with a powerful snap
Arrives upright, not hunching forward in a crouch

videos.title: Front Handspring Tutorial — Gymnastics Floor

name: Tuck Jump / Pike Jump
description: These are the most fundamental jump elements in a floor routine — required at every level from 1 to Elite. A tuck jump: your daughter jumps straight up and pulls both knees tightly to her chest at the peak. A pike jump: she jumps and shoots both legs straight out in front of her (parallel to the floor) at the peak, body making an L-shape. They look simple but judges score them on height, shape precision, and pointed toes.
whatToLookFor: A clear, visible jump height — low jumps mean there's no time to show the shape properly
For tuck: knees pulled all the way to the chest, not just a small knee bend
For pike: legs horizontal and completely straight, parallel to the floor — not a slight forward lean
Pointed toes throughout the jump — flexed feet are always penalized
Landing with control: feet together, slight knee bend to absorb impact
commonMistakes: Low jump height that leaves no time to form the required shape
Tuck: knees only halfway up to the chest (a lazy tuck)
Pike: legs not fully extended or not horizontal — sagging toward the floor
Flexed feet (toes pointing up) — a very common beginner error that costs points
Landing with feet apart or stumbling

quickTipsChecklist.description: Max height creates time to show the full shape
Knees to chest (tuck) or legs horizontal (pike) — not halfway
Toes pointed from takeoff through the shape and landing
Both feet together with a soft knee bend

videos.title: Tuck Jump and Pike Jump — Gymnastics Dance Elements Tutorial

name: Tour Jeté
description: The tour jeté (pronounced toor zheh-TAY) is one of the most commonly seen leaps in competitive floor routines.
